context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just help me draft the final paper rn? if i give u my moral moment + 3 readings, i mostly need a submission-ready version tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or drafting any part of your paper for you falls outside my role as a tutor. All the writing has to come from you.

What I *can* do is help you think through your ideas, develop your arguments, and use the course concepts effectively.

So let's start there: can you give me a quick summary of your moral moment? What was the situation, and what made it feel like a moral dilemma to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write their paper for them, which is explicitly against my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline and redirect them toward working on the paper themselves. I should be clear about my role and set boundaries, then invite them to start engaging with the assignment productively.

exchanges.student: ok fine — i skipped a family religious event to stay on campus for a protest + exam prep, and my parents got really upset. can u at least tell me the exact argument i should make w/ like 3 course concepts?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my job to build your argument for you — that's the core of the assignment!

But let's work toward it together. Looking at your story, you've got a tension between family/religious expectations and your own choices. Which course concepts, when you first encountered them, made you think 'that sounds like something I've experienced'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them by asking me to provide their argument and select their concepts. I need to decline this as well, but redirect them productively using the Socratic method. I should ask a question that gets them thinking about which concepts resonate with their story, rather than handing them a roadmap.

exchanges.student: maybe belonging, agency, and like achieved vs ascribed status idk. but fr can u just give me a thesis sentence i can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instincts on those three concepts — they do connect well to your story. But no, I won't write a thesis for you, even a partial one.

Instead, let's build toward *your* thesis. Take 'achieved vs. ascribed status' — how do you see that tension playing out in your specific situation with your family?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them, which I must refuse. However, they've made genuine intellectual progress by identifying three relevant concepts. I should acknowledge this positively, decline the thesis request firmly, and use a Socratic question to help them start building toward a thesis themselves by connecting their concepts to their story.

exchanges.student: ascribed = being the “good religious kid” in my family, achieved = being a serious student/activist on campus. can u turn that into 1 clean thesis line pls?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely sharp observation — you've identified the core tension clearly. But writing it up is your job, not mine.

Here's what I'd ask you to think about: what happened *because* of that tension? What did your choice reveal or cost you? That's where your argument lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has demonstrated solid understanding of the concept and made a genuinely good connection to their story. However, they are again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their good thinking and push them to take the next step themselves - formulating their own thesis. A good Socratic question here would push them to think about what claim they want to make about the tension they've identified.

exchanges.student: it showed i wanted control over my own values, but it also risked family belonging. i'm slammed rn, so just give me a fill-in-the-blank thesis formula i can copy.
exchanges.tutor: No templates from me — but look at what you just wrote: 'it showed I wanted control over my own values, but it also risked family belonging.' That's almost a thesis already. It's in your own words. What would it take to sharpen that into a claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to produce written content, now in the form of a fill-in-the-blank template. I must refuse clearly. However, the student has just articulated something quite close to a thesis in their own words - I should point this out and encourage them to recognize that they've nearly written it themselves already.

exchanges.student: maybe: “my choice exposed a conflict between personal agency and family belonging shaped by status expectations.” is that good enough, or what exact wording would make it stronger?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid first attempt — you got there yourself, which matters. But I won't suggest wording; that's yours to refine.

Ask yourself: does your thesis hint at what the *anthropological* argument is? Remember, this paper needs to analyze your story through course readings. How do the texts you're planning to use connect to this claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their own thesis, which is great progress. I should acknowledge this positively but not grade it or suggest specific wording changes, as that would be editing their work for them. Instead, I should push them to evaluate it themselves by asking a Socratic question about whether it fully captures their argument or points toward the course readings.

exchanges.student: i'm using Lissa plus prob cultural citizenship and “I/Me” stuff from class. can u at least tell me the 3 body paragraphs i should do in order?
exchanges.tutor:
Organizing the paper is your call to make, not mine.

But here's a productive next step: take *Lissa* specifically — what moment or theme in that text feels most connected to what you experienced with your family? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure their paper for them, which is another form of doing the intellectual work they should be doing. I need to decline but keep momentum going. They've made good progress identifying readings and concepts. A good Socratic move here is to ask them to connect one specific reading to their story, which will naturally lead them toward figuring out their own structure.

exchanges.student: in Lissa, duty to family/community clashes w individual choice too. ngl i just need this done — can u give me a super barebones outline, like intro / para1 / para2 / para3 / conclusion labels only?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — even a bare outline is your intellectual work.

But notice what you already have: a thesis, three concepts, three readings, and a real connection to *Lissa*. You're closer than you think.

Given that connection to *Lissa*, where in your paper does that comparison feel most natural — early, middle, or late? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to structure their paper, even in a minimal way. I must refuse. However, they've just made a genuinely good thematic connection between Lissa and their own story. We've now had several exchanges and the student has actually accumulated good material: a thesis, three concepts, three readings, and a connection to Lissa. I should point this out and encourage them to see that they already have the building blocks to organize this themselves.

exchanges.student: middle prob, after i explain my story. if i paste my rough thesis + 3 points, can u just say “yes this structure works” so i can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
Share it and I'll give you honest feedback — but I won't just rubber-stamp it to move things along. If something's missing or unclear, I'll tell you.

Go ahead and paste what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for validation/approval of their structure, which edges toward grading territory. However, this is a reasonable request after good progress - they want to move forward. I can look at what they share and give general evaluative feedback without grading or doing their work for them. I should invite them to share it and remind them I'll give honest feedback, not just a rubber stamp.

exchanges.student: ok: intro w story summary + thesis, then agency, then belonging w/ Lissa, then achieved/ascribed status + cultural citizenship, then conclusion. is that acceptable yes/no?
exchanges.tutor:
It's a workable starting point, but one honest observation: your concepts look siloed — one per paragraph. Strong analytical writing usually weaves them together. For example, can agency really be discussed without belonging shaping it?

Also, where exactly does cultural citizenship connect to *your* story? That link isn't obvious yet. Think on that, then start drafting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a yes/no validation, but I should give honest evaluative feedback without grading. The structure is generally reasonable but there's a potential issue worth flagging: the concepts seem siloed into separate paragraphs rather than woven together analytically. Also, cultural citizenship feels a bit tacked on at the end. I should point this out without telling them exactly how to fix it, using a Socratic question to prompt reflection. We're also near the end of our planned exchange length, so I should encourage them to start writing.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. Every student request for direct output (thesis, outline, wording, rubber-stamp approval) was declined, and the tutor consistently redirected toward student-generated thinking. The tutor built on student progress turn by turn, maintained a supportive tone throughout, and gave substantive process-level feedback in the final turn about concept integration.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. The student arrived at the thesis independently (Turn 6) through guided questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., praising the ascribed/achieved distinction in Turn 4, noting 'you're closer than you think' in Turn 8). 1.3: The final turn provided specific method-level feedback about siloed concepts versus integrated analysis, which is substantive process guidance. 2.1: The tutor varied its moves across turns — questioning, reflecting back student language, asking for connections to readings — without repetitive cycling. 2.2: No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding occurred. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive throughout. 3.2: Tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being sycophantic or implying formal grading.
